perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon notice from either party that it does not wish to proceed with the Transaction, each party shall return to the other party or destroy all copies of the Evaluation Material (including all documents based thereon) in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives, except that outside counsel to the receiving party may retain one copy of the Evaluation Material for archival purposes and solely to comply with applicable law, rule or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon notice from either party that it does not wish to proceed with the Transaction, each party shall return to the other party or destroy all copies of the Evaluation Material (including all documents based thereon) in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives, except that outside counsel to the receiving party may retain one copy of the Evaluation Material for archival purposes and solely to comply with applicable law, rule or regulation. There is no requirement to promptly return or destroy copies of evaluation material.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'promptly' requirement and stating there is no requirement to promptly return the documents contradicts California law regarding the return of confidential information after the termination of an agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires the return or destruction of confidential information after the termination of an agreement. By stating there is no requirement to promptly return the documents, the modified text contradicts this legal requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.3 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.3. (a) A complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (b) If neither damages nor unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation are provable, the court may order payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subdivision (a) or (b). (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'prompt' action regarding evaluation material, but the changed text explicitly states 'There is no requirement to promptly return or destroy copies of evaluation material.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both claim this contradicts California law related to returning or destroying confidential information, implying a timely return is legally expected. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state the time frame for returning confidential information, it does discuss damages for misappropriation which implies an expectation of return of confidential information. Given the change directly contradicts the original promptness requirement, and the explanation claims this violates California law, there is a contradiction. The absence of explicit timing in the snippet does not negate the contradiction within the agreement itself as the agreement now states there is NO requirement to promptly return materials.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all rights and obligations hereunder shall terminate on the second anniversary of the Effective Date. Promptly upon notice from either party that it does not wish to proceed with the Transaction, each party shall return to the other party or destroy all copies of the Evaluation Material (including all documents based thereon) in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives, except that outside counsel to the receiving party may retain one copy of the Evaluation Material for archival purposes and solely to comply with applicable law, rule or regulation. Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder for a period ending on the second anniversary of the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all rights and obligations hereunder shall terminate on the second anniversary of the Effective Date. Promptly upon notice from either party that it does not wish to proceed with the Transaction, each party shall return to the other party or destroy all copies of the Evaluation Material (including all documents based thereon) in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives, except that outside counsel to the receiving party may retain one copy of the Evaluation Material for archival purposes and solely to comply with applicable law, rule or regulation. Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality obligation to 'indefinitely' after the termination date could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. An indefinite confidentiality obligation could be construed as such a restraint, especially if it prevents a party from using general knowledge or skills acquired during the evaluation process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text changes the confidentiality obligation from a defined period (two years after the effective date) to 'indefinitely'. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), which states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' An indefinite confidentiality obligation could prevent a party from using general knowledge acquired during the evaluation process, thus restraining them from engaging in their profession, trade, or business. The indefinite restraint created by the modified text is a direct conflict with the law's intent to prevent contracts that restrain trade.

perturbation.original_text: Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted exclusively in any state or federal court In San Francisco or San Mateo County, California. Company and Oracle irrevocably and unconditionally agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of, and agree that the venue is proper in, the aforesaid courts in any such legal action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted exclusively in any court In San Francisco or San Mateo County, California, at Oracle's sole discretion. Company and Oracle irrevocably and unconditionally agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of, and agree that the venue is proper in, the aforesaid courts in any such legal action or proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: Granting Oracle sole discretion to choose any court in San Francisco or San Mateo County could be viewed as an unenforceable forum selection clause under California law if it's deemed unreasonable or unfairly obtained.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California generally enforces forum selection clauses, they are unenforceable if unreasonable. Giving one party sole discretion over the court choice raises concerns about fairness and could be deemed unreasonable, especially if the chosen forum is significantly inconvenient for the other party.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article 2. Forum - Sections 410.30-410.42 :: California Code of Civil Procedure :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Code of Civil Procedure Sections 410.30-410.42 Article 2. Forum CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 410.30-410.42 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. 410.40. Any person may maintain an action or proceeding in a court of this state against a foreign corporation or nonresident person where the action or proceeding arises out of or relates to any contract, agreement, or undertaking for which a choice of California law has been made in whole or in part by the parties thereto and which (a) is a contract, agreement, or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, relating to a transaction involving in the aggregate not less than one million dollars ($1,000,000), and (b) contains a provision or provisions under which the foreign corporation or nonresident agrees to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state. This section applies to contracts, agreements, and undertakings entered into before, on, or after its effective date; it shall be fully retroactive. Contracts, agreements, and undertakings selecting California law entered into before the effective date of this section shall be valid, enforceable, and effective as if this section had been in effect on the date they were entered into; and actions and proceedings commencing in a court of this state before the effecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text gives Oracle sole discretion in choosing a court within San Francisco or San Mateo County. This raises concerns about fairness, potentially making the forum selection clause unenforceable under California law if deemed unreasonable, as the Law Explanation suggests. While the scraped snippets don't directly address 'sole discretion', they imply that forum selection can be challenged if not in the interest of justice (CCP 410.30(a)). This introduces an inconsistency, as the original text had both parties agree, while the changed text creates a power imbalance. Therefore, the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition applies, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the procedural and substantive laws of the state of California and shall be deemed executed in Redwood City, California. Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted exclusively in any state or federal court In San Francisco or San Mateo County, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Delaware and shall be deemed executed in Delaware. Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted exclusively in any court In San Francisco or San Mateo County, California.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Delaware law while mandating California courts might be unenforceable if Delaware law significantly disadvantages one party concerning a contract executed and performed primarily in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states a contract must be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of that place. By forcing governing law as Delaware but requiring all action to take place in California the contract creates uncertainty and may become unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law while mandating that any legal action takes place in California. This creates a contradiction under California Civil Code Section 1646, which states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed. The original text specified California law and California courts. The change introduces uncertainty by applying Delaware law to a contract performed in California, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable as described in the Law Explanation. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, because it creates an inconsistency in the application of laws.
